coredata.dc:title: Microstructural evolution and formation mechanism of FCC titanium hydride in Ti–6Al–4V–xH alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shan, D.B.
Zong, Y.Y.
Lu, T.F.
Lv, Y.
coredata.dc:description: The microstructural evolution and phase transformations in the hydrogenated (0–0.5wt.%) Ti6Al4V alloy were studied to reveal the influence of hydrogen on the characteristics of titanium alloys. The crystallographic, microstructural and formation mechanism for hydride behavior were investigated. Conclusive evidences for the face-centered cubic (FCC) δ hydride phase formation were found in the Ti6Al4V alloy by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The δ hydrides could precipitate not only from the α phase, but also from the β phase. The formation mechanisms of the δ hydrides formed in both phases were established. The equilibrium phase transformation sequence for the hydrogenated Ti6Al4V alloy during cooling was presented.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti6Al4V alloy
FCC δ hydride
Phase transformations
Formation mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discussion on the formation mechanism of the hydride

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microstructure of the starting material: (a) TEM image showing α+β phase structure; (b) SAED pattern of the interface phase.
Microstructure of the Ti6Al4V alloy: (a) starting specimen; (b) hydrogenated 0.2wt.%; (c) hydrogenated 0.3wt.%; (d) hydrogenated 0.5wt.%.
XRD patterns of the Ti6Al4V alloy: (a) before hydrogenation; (b) hydrogenated 0.2wt.%; (c) hydrogenated 0.3wt.%; (d) hydrogenated 0.5wt.%.
TEM image of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2wt.% hydrogen: (a) α lamellar structure; (b) δ hydride formed in α lath; (c) corresponding SAED pattern of hydride.
TEM image of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.3wt.% hydrogen: (a) parallel hydride laths; (b) SAED pattern of parallel hydrides; (c) hydrides formed in α and β phases.

TEM image of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.5wt.% hydrogen: (a) parallel hydrides; (b) corresponding SAED pattern of hydride and α lamella; (c) twins in hydrides; (d) corresponding SAED pattern shown [011]δ,

; (e) integral pattern; (f) faults in coarse α lath.

Schematic diagram for the formation mechanism of δ hydride: βH →α+δ.
Schematic diagrams illustrating the microstructural evolution and phase transformations of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen during cooling.

article.ce:title: Microstructural evolution and formation mechanism of FCC titanium hydride in Ti–6Al–4V–xH alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The microstructural evolution and phase transformations in the hydrogenated (0–0.5wt.%) Ti6Al4V alloy were studied to reveal the influence of hydrogen on the characteristics of titanium alloys. The crystallographic, microstructural and formation mechanism for hydride behavior were investigated. Conclusive evidences for the face-centered cubic (FCC) δ hydride phase formation were found in the Ti6Al4V alloy by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The δ hydrides could precipitate not only from the α phase, but also from the β phase. The formation mechanisms of the δ hydrides formed in both phases were established. The equilibrium phase transformation sequence for the hydrogenated Ti6Al4V alloy during cooling was presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium alloys have a large affinity for hydrogen, the addition of which has a considerable effect on their microstructures and mechanical properties [1,2]. At near-room temperatures, hydrogen causes embrittlement of titanium alloys because of the formation and subsequent brittle fracture of hydrides, which leads to a degradation in fracture-related mechanical properties [3]; on the other hand, hydrogen embrittlement provides an economic method for the production of titanium powder at high hydrogen concentration [4]. At elevated temperatures, hydrogen as a temporary alloying element in titanium alloys could refine the microstructure and improve the properties of the alloys due to the hydrogen enhanced-plasticity, hydrogen-induced phase transformations and the revisable reaction of hydrogen with titanium [5–7]. Consequently, materials scientists are interested in the hydrogen alloying behavior in titanium alloys.

Three types of titanium hydrides have been reported: face-centered tetragonal (FCT) (c/a >1) γ hydride, FCC δ hydride and FCT (c/a <1) ɛ hydride with the increase of the hydrogen content [8]. Much work has been worked on the γ hydride [9,10] and the δ hydride formed in α titanium alloys [11,12]. However, the microstructural features of the δ hydride in α/β titanium alloys are still not thoroughly investigated, especially for the δ hydride formed in the β phase. The aim of this paper is to study the effects of hydrogen on the microstructural evolution of the hydrogenated Ti6Al4V alloy and the hydrides formed in both α and β phases.

The material chosen for this study was a Ti6Al4V alloy, which consisted of α phase, transformed β microstructure and an interface phase formed at α and β phase boundaries, as shown in Fig. 1a. The selected-area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) of the interface suggested FCC structure, as shown in Fig. 1b. The phase transformation temperature of the starting alloy was around 1273K. Specimens were hydrogenated at 1123K for 1.5h under 1atm of hydrogenation treatment and then given furnace cooling to 873K followed by air-cooling to room temperature. Specimens with various amounts of hydrogen, in the range of 0.2–0.5wt.% were attained by controlling the hydrogen pressure.

Phase compositions and lattice parameters were measured by XRD using Cu Kα radiation at 40kV and 40mA. XRD was performed on the Ti6Al4V bars as well as the hydrogenated specimens using a D/Max-RB rotating anode X-ray diffractometer. The microstructural evolution was investigated by optical microscopy (OM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). TEM samples were prepared by electropolishing in a solution of 6vol.% HClO4 +34vol.% C4H9OH+60vol.% CH3OH. TEM observations were carried out on a Philips CM-12 microscope operated at 120kV.

The microstructures of the Ti6Al4V alloy containing different hydrogen contents are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the starting Ti6Al4V specimen had an equiaxed α+β dual phase structure, with an average prior-α grain size of 19μm and an equiaxed α phase area fraction of 85% (Fig. 2a). After hydrogenation, needle α phase appeared in the specimens. Twenty-four percent prior equiaxed α phase retained when 0.2wt.% hydrogen was charged in the alloy. However, the α grain size decreased to 14μm due to the α→β phase transformation via α/β interfacial migration. Since the growth of lamella α was restricted by the boundaries of the prior equiaxed α grains, the α lamellas in this specimen were relatively short (Fig. 2b). The existence of the retained equiaxed α grains indicates that the specimen containing 0.2wt.% hydrogen is hydrogenated below the β-transus temperature. The specimens containing 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen had a coarse α lath structure and a fine needle α martensite structure with clear β grain boundaries, respectively. The two specimens had only needle α structure, and no equiaxed α grain was detected. The formation of needle α phase indicates that the two specimens are conducted in a single-phase β region or above the β-transus temperature. It was considered that hydrogen decreases the α→β phase transformation temperature because the hydrogen stabilizes the β phase. And these results are consistent with the reference data [13]. The morphology of the α structure is dependent on its formation temperature which is evaluated by the alloying composition and hydrogen content. The alloying composition was the same in this study and thus the hydrogen content was important in determining the α structure. Since an increase in the hydrogen content lowered the martensite start temperature of the Ti6Al4V alloy, the supercooling increased with increasing hydrogen content and, in turn, fine needle α formed, as shown in Fig. 2d.

The XRD patterns of Ti6Al4V alloy containing different hydrogen are shown in Fig. 3 , respectively. The XRD pattern indicates that the starting Ti6Al4V specimen contains primary α phase and β phase. After hydrogenation, XRD patterns changed obviously. In this paper, the peaks of titanium hydride appeared and only suggested δ phase, which was confirmed by the TEM as would be discussed later. The results could be indexed using the crystal structure of the FCC δ hydride phase having a lattice parameter of 0.444nm. The δ hydride forms in the composition range from TiH1.5 to TiH2 and has a CaF2 type structure with titanium atoms on an FCC lattice and hydrogen atoms randomly occupying tetrahedral interstitial sites [14]. Moreover, the relative intensities of the δ peaks increased gradually with increasing hydrogen content. There was no evidence of the existence of the FCT (c/a >l) γ hydride phase. This was because according to the microscopy microstructure, the Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen at 1123K were already in the β phase region from which only δ FCC hydride forms. The Ti6Al4V alloy hydrogenated with 0.2wt.% at 1123K was in the α+β phase region from which a mixture of both FCT and FCC hydrides forms [8]. The γ hydride precipitates in α-Ti containing very low (1–3at.%) hydrogen. The specimens investigated in this paper contained considerably much more hydrogen (0.2–0.5wt.%, in accordance with 9.7–19at.%), so only medium hydrogen-bearing FCC hydride formed. The formation of the δ phase led to the broadening of some α phase peaks due to the overlapping of α and δ phase peaks. The other reason for the peak widening was evidently caused by the presence of the hexagonal α′ martensite, with lattice parameters differing only slightly from that of the α phase because of the composition variations in these phases. The peaks moved to the lower angles because of the lattice expansion of α and β phases with the solution of hydrogen atoms.

More detailed microstructural information is given by TEM. The TEM photographs of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5wt.% hydrogen are shown in Figs. 4–6
, respectively. As can be seen that hydride can precipitate not only from HCP α lath but also from BCC β phase.

The specimen exhibited a mainly α lamellar structure with retained β present at colony boundaries (Fig. 4a). Additional, Fig. 4b suggests that hydride phase can nucleate directly from the HCP α lath. The hydride plates formed inside alpha lath and grew from the phase boundary into the α matrix. The corresponding SAED pattern suggested FCC structure for the δ hydride with [001] zone axis (Fig. 4c).

Fig. 5 shows the TEM images of the specimen containing 0.3wt.% hydrogen. It can be seen that δ hydrides can precipitate from not only α phase but also β phase. Fig. 5a is an image showing the α lamellar structure with retained beta present at colony boundaries and in the retained beta phase δ lamellar structure with α plates present at δ boundaries. The δ lamellar hydrides formed in β phase showed FCC crystal structure with
[
1 ¯
1
2 ]
zone axis, shown in Fig. 5b and the hydride formed in α lath with [011] zone axis the same with Fig. 4c. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that hydrides in specimen hydrogenated with 0.3wt.% were far more than that in specimen hydrogenated with 0.2wt.%.

Fig. 6a is an image showing the specimen containing 0.5wt.% hydrogen also exhibited a mainly lamellar structure, although there are more extensive δ hydrides than in the specimen containing 0.3wt.% hydrogen. The corresponding SAED pattern is shown in Fig. 6b, from which the orientation relationship between δ and α phases could be directed identified as:

(220)δ. Fig. 6c shows the microscopy of the FCC δ hydride twins and Fig. 5d is the corresponding SAED pattern, which shows the diffraction zones of [011]δ and

. Fig. 6d also reveals the orientation relationship of adjacent twinned regions to be

)
, which is in fair accordance with the literature [5] data. Fig. 6e shows that coarsened α lamellae penetrated the selected region and divided the region into several different regions where fine martensites with different orientations formed. There was no orthorhombic α″ found in this study, but HCP α′ martensite existed. Additionally, high densities of planar faults were observed in the coarse α laths, shown in Fig. 6f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on the formation mechanism of the hydride
ce:section.ce:para: The formation mechanism of hydride is the diffusion of atoms. This may be attributed to follow reasons: (1) Hydrogen atoms diffuse easily in titanium alloys. The hydrogen diffusivity of 4.96×10−13 cm2/s at 298K can be obtained from the equation D =3.0×10−2 exp(−14.7/RT) where R is in kcal/molK and T is temperature [15]. (2) The addition of hydrogen weakens the interatomic interaction of matrix, especially for the high hydrogen content (0.2–0.5wt.%) in this study [16]. (3) The composition difference between hydride and parent phase is large, which shear mechanism cannot realize [17]. Hydrides could precipitate in the retained βH phase and directly from the α lath during cooling from 1123K. So we separately discuss the two kinds of formation mechanisms of hydrides in α and β phases.

According to the Ti–H phase diagram [18], as shown in Fig. 7 , titanium containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen, beta phase decomposed partially into thick alpha lath and a mainly α lamellar structure with retained β present at colony boundaries forms during cooling from 1123 to 592K. Since the hydrogenation of titanium is an exothermic reaction, the cooling process promotes the hydrogenation of titanium [19]. However, the solid solubility of hydrogen in titanium alloys is certain, during cooling of the hydrogenated alloy to a temperature below 592K, the δ phase precipitates from both supersaturated unstable α and β phases. However, the supersaturation of hydrogen occurs firstly in the α laths due to the smaller solid solubility of hydrogen in α-Ti compared to β-Ti. On the other hand, because the volume misfit for the β→δ transition (8–11%) is quite larger than that for α→δ transition (0–5%) [20], it is hard for the crystal structure to transform from BCC into FCC. So the titanium hydride precipitates firstly in the α laths, as shown in Fig. 4. The driving force of the hydride formation is the distortion of lattice caused by the solid solution of hydrogen in α phase. It appears that the formation of the δ phase is the result of a phase separation reaction: αH →α(H-lean)+δ(H-rich). As a result of a phase separation reaction in which the α phase containing hydrogen separates into hydrogen rich and hydrogen lean regions, hydrogen rich regions transform to the δ phase. In fact, the decomposition of the alpha phase into α+δ lamellae is shown in Fig. 4a. Therefore, the δ phase may be considered as a pseudo-hexagonal phase with a lattice distortion resulting from a supersaturation in the α phase. To accommodate the excess hydrogen content, the supersaturated α lattice is no longer able to maintain its hexagonal symmetry and becomes a δ lattice. That is, αH →α(H-lean)+δ(H-rich).

The retained βH phase (with hydrogen), which can resolve a great deal of hydrogen (up to 45at.% at 592K), exists between the α laths. When the hydrogenated specimens cooled to 592K, the eutectoid reaction βH →α+δ takes place. During the eutectoid reaction, the formations of δ and α phases undergo a diffusion-controlled nucleation and growth. The δ phase prefers to precipitate at interfaces/interphases. This may be due to: (1) Around hydrogen atoms and interfaces/interphases both exist a strain field. These two kinds of strain fields may interact with each other, which lead to hydrogen atoms accumulated at interfaces/interphases. Thus, the density of hydrogen at interfaces/interphases is very high, which supplies the concentration fluctuation for the hydride formation. (2) The density of dislocation and defect is high and atoms are easy to diffuse at the boundaries, which supply the fluctuation of energy and structure for the hydride formation. In the eutectoid reaction, δ and α phases grow into the β phase with the lamellar structure after nucleation at boundaries, as shown in schematic diagram Fig. 8 .

Due to the larger volume misfit between the hydride and matrix, lattice distortion of the matrix accompanies with the precipitation of hydride. The strain energy as a result of the lattice distortion will be accommodated elastically or plastically, such as the plastic deformation in the adjacent Ti matrix or the twinning of hydride (Fig. 6f). High compression stress as a result of hydrogen gradient, which can lead to plastic deformation of superficial material, occurs on the superficial coat of the hydrogenated specimen. The local stress can lead to the production of a martensite structure in the hydrogenated specimens after cooling in air. Thus the needle α martensite precipitation β→α′ occurs. This kind of hydrogen inducing martensite transformation can be seen as processing inducing martensite transformation. Therefore, the equilibrium phase transformation sequence for the Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen during cooling is β→αlath ⇒αH →α+δ⇒βH →α+δ⇒β→α′. The corresponding schematic diagrams illustrating the microstructural evolution and phase transformations of Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen during cooling are shown in Fig. 9 .

The microstructural evolution and phase transformations were studied in the hydrogenated (0–0.5wt.%) Ti6Al4V alloy. The α→β phase transformation temperature decreases and the α lath structure becomes finer with increasing of hydrogen content. The FCC δ hydrides precipitate in the Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen and the hydrides increase with the increase of hydrogen content. There is no orthorhombic α″ found in this study, but hexagonal-close-packed (HCP) α′ phase exists. The formation of δ hydride involving nucleation and growth is a diffusion mechanism. The hydrides with a lamellar structure can form in the residual βH phase by eutectoid reaction. The δ hydrides can also directly precipitate in α laths. The equilibrium phase transformation sequence for the Ti6Al4V alloy containing 0.2–0.5wt.% hydrogen during cooling is β→αlath ⇒αH →α+δ⇒βH →α+δ⇒β→α′.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank vice professor Qingchang Meng for useful discussions and technical assistance. The services of the Materials Analysis Department of Harbin Institute of Technology are greatly appreciated.
